purpose: Author/title/identifier search-key matrix for the Haqiqi witness census. The matrix combines the modern-academic baseline with 19th-c. Orientalist forms, Soviet/Azerbaijani query forms, and additional Arabic-script orthographic variants. Extended 2026-05-11 with a `script_families` block covering 9 additional script-tradition variants (Russian Cyrillic modern, Azerbaijani Latin/Cyrillic modern + Soviet, Georgian Mkhedruli, Uzbek Latin/Cyrillic, Tajik Cyrillic, Devanagari, Urdu nastaʿliq) for institutional catalogues in the Russian Federation, Caucasus, Central Asia, and South Asia.

nineteenth_century_orientalist._note: How Rieu, Blochet, Pertsch, Ethé, and other 19th-c. cataloguers would have rendered the name.

soviet_russian_azerbaijani._note: Forms used in Soviet-era cataloguing systems; relevant for Yerevan/Baku/Tashkent/Dushanbe holdings.

arabic_script._note: Multiple orthographic variants reflect cataloguer drift; both the dotted-yā and dotless-ya variants (حقىقى vs حقیقی) are attested.

catalogue_context_keys._note: Catalogue-context terms used to find Haqiqi witnesses indexed under generic descriptors rather than author. Search these AND/OR with author+title forms when the author-direct query returns zero.
european_catalogue_descriptors: recueil de poésies persanes et turques

scribe_and_colophon_keys._note: Scribe-name search keys: may find sibling manuscripts by the same scribe.

nesimi_adjacency_keys._note: Nesîmî/Hurufi adjacency mining keys for mecmûʿa sweeps. Haqiqi poems demonstrably travel inside Nesîmî collections (Ankara MK A 5252 evidence).

modern_editions_and_studies_for_backchai._note: Edition backchain keys. For each editor/author: which manuscript(s) used as base text? Cited as variants? Saw firsthand? Includes editions plus the Demirci 1997 article/study that introduced Süleymaniye Fatih 3808 + Ankara DTCF I/2221.

editions_and_studies.note: Article/study (not a critical edition). Introduced Süleymaniye Fatih 3808 + Ankara DTCF I/2221 as the iki yeni nüsha; see Macit 2000 verbatim citation.

142 + 1 pp; 15 cm; Cyrillic; Klassik Azərbaycan ədəbiyyatı series. Per ek.anl.az 2026-05-11 round, this is the 1986 Bakı Yazıçı 'Seçilmiş əsərləri' edition; v1.0 deposit conflated it with the 2006 Şərq-Qərb edition (now split out below).

editions_and_studies.editor: Rahimov, Hüseynzadə & Hacı Mail (red. M. Mailoğlu)
editions_and_studies.note: 152 pp; 15 cm; Klassik Azərbaycan ədəbiyyatı series. Distinct from the 1986 Yazıçı edition (different publisher Şərq-Qərb, different editorial team Hacı Mail replacing Əliyev). v1.0.1 correction F-V1-CORRECTION-2 via ek.anl.az probe 2026-05-11.

Qəzəllər və rübailər; 109 + 1 pp; 14 cm; Arabic script (NOT Cyrillic). Editors Rəhimov + Əliyev without Hüseyinzade. Arabic-script transliteration target is likely Iranian-Azerbaijani diaspora audience. F-EDITION3 via ek.anl.az probe 2026-05-11.

Şeirlər; 103 + 4 pp; 17 cm; Cyrillic. v1.0 deposit attributes this 1966 Yerevan edition to Hüseyinzade as compiler/editor; ek.anl.az records 'Ə. Yerevanlı' as redaktor. Possible Soviet editorial-role distinction (Yerevanlı=redaktor / Hüseynzadə=tərtibçi). 8 copies held at ANL Baku; ILL pathway. F-V1-CORRECTION-1.

'Cahanşah Həqiqi' chapter in 'Keçmişimizdən gələn səslər. II buraxılış'. S. 3-4-47. Pre-dates 1986 Bakı Yazıçı edition by 4 years; potentially the source for 1986 base text. F-EDITION2a via manuscript.az probe 2026-05-11.

Newspaper article on Cahanşah Həqiqi dated 4 iyun 1987. F-EDITION2c via manuscript.az probe 2026-05-11. Brief popular-history piece; cited as evidence of Az popular reception, not as critical edition.

Persian poems edition; second/updated edition by the same editor. Surfaced 2026-05-11 via ISAM Yordam direct GET-form exercise (chunk C2 finding F-EDITION1); not in v1.0 deposit. Add to backchain matrix for v2.0 textual-criticism collation work.

script_families._note: Additive 2026-05-11 extension covering 9 script families for institutional-catalogue indexing in Russian Federation, Caucasus, Central Asia, and South Asia repositories. Complementary to `author_forms` above: that block is organized by language/scholarly tradition (modern academic Latin, 19th-c. Orientalist, Soviet-Cyrillic, Arabic-script); this block is organized by script with applies_to/cihansah_variants/hakiki_variants/qaraqoyunlu_variants/indexing_notes per family. Some overlap exists at the Soviet-Cyrillic intersection (e.g. Хакики, Cahanşah Həqiqi appear in both axes); this is by design (the two organizational views complement rather than replace each other).
_transliteration_sources_cross_checked: Encyclopaedia of Islam 3rd edition (EI3); standard scholarly transliteration of Persian/Arabic/Turkic names
GOST 7.79 / ISO 9; Russian Cyrillic transliteration standards
TÜBİTAK ULAKBİM / Türkiye Bilimler Akademisi; Modern Turkish and Azerbaijani Latin orthography standards
Tbilisi State University Caucasiology Institute conventions; Georgian Mkhedruli transliteration of Persian/Turkic proper names
ALA-LC romanization tables; used by US research library catalogues for South Asian Persian/Urdu manuscripts
script_families._caveat: These variants are hand-constructed from standard transliteration rules and cross-checked against the above references, but local catalogue indexing may use orthographic conventions not anticipated here. Encountering an unfamiliar variant in a portal hit should NOT be treated as a false positive without further inspection.

russian_cyrillic_modern.description: Russian Federation institutional catalogues (IOM-RAS St. Petersburg, RGB Moscow, KFU Kazan, National Library of Tatarstan). Cyrillic alphabet, post-1918 orthography. Persian/Turkic proper names transliterated following GOST 7.79 or Russian Iranian-studies conventions.
indexing_notes: Russian academic Persianist convention typically uses hyphen between compound proper-name elements (Кара-Коюнлу, Джахан-Шах).
Some Soviet-era publications dropped the hyphen (Каракоюнлу, Джеханшах).
Pen-name Хакики ('Khakiki' in romanized Russian) is the standard form; the variant Хакыки uses ы for the Persian /iː/.

azerbaijani_latin_modern.description: Modern Azerbaijani Latin orthography (post-1991 official), used in AMEA / Baku Institute of Manuscripts and Azerbaijan National Library current catalogues. Uses ə for schwa, ş for /ʃ/, ç for /tʃ/, q for /ɣ/-like; uses i / ı distinction (dotted vs dotless).
indexing_notes: Modern Azerbaijani uses Ş for /ʃ/ where Turkish modern uses Ş; both forms map to Persian ش.
Modern Azerbaijani uses Q for /q/ where Persian uses ق; this Q is identical to the Azerbaijani Cyrillic Г-with-bar-equivalent.
Azerbaijani specifically marks schwa ə in Həqiqi where Turkish would use a or Persian e; this is the primary distinguishing feature.

azerbaijani_cyrillic_soviet.description: Soviet-era Azerbaijani Cyrillic (1939-1991, with partial use to ~2001 and in some legacy catalogue records). Distinct from Russian Cyrillic in adding letters Ҹ (for /dʒ/), Ј (for /j/), Ҝ (for /ɟ/), Ғ (for /ɣ/), Ә (for schwa), Ү (for /y/), Ө (for /œ/), Һ (for /h/).
indexing_notes: Soviet-era Azerbaijani Cyrillic differs from Russian Cyrillic in its use of Ҹ (J + cedilla) for /dʒ/, where Russian uses Дж digraph.
Soviet-era Az Cyrillic uses Г for /q/ (so Қарақоюнлу becomes Гарагојунлу), distinct from Russian Cyrillic which used К-К compound (Кара-Коюнлу).
Records published 1939-1991 in Azerbaijan use this script; records 1991-present use Latin (azerbaijani_latin_modern).

georgian_mkhedruli.description: Georgian alphabet (Mkhedruli script), used at Kekelidze Institute of Manuscripts (Tbilisi), Tbilisi State University, Georgian National Center of Manuscripts. Persian/Turkic proper names transliterated following Georgian phonetics with -i nominative suffix typical for foreign proper names ending in consonant.
indexing_notes: Georgian alphabet does not distinguish case; all letters are essentially the same single-case Mkhedruli script.
Georgian uses ჰ for /h/ and ხ for /x/; the distinction between Persian ح / ه is sometimes neutralized to ჰ.
Georgian uses ყ for /q/ (a deep glottal-stop-like sound), distinct from კ /k/.
Persian Hakîkî (حقیقی) renders as ჰაკიკი if treating ح as /h/ and ق as /k/; alternative ჰაყიყი uses ყ for ق and double-ი for the long /iː/.

uzbek_latin_modern.description: Modern Uzbek Latin orthography (1995-present, gradually replacing Cyrillic in Uzbekistan). Used in Tashkent Al-Biruni Institute and Beruni Institute of Oriental Studies catalogues for newer entries. Uses o instead of u for /o/-vowel; uses sh / ch digraphs; uses q for /q/ and gʻ for /ɣ/.
indexing_notes: Uzbek Latin uses 'o' where Persian uses long ā (Jahonshoh for Persian Jahānshāh).
Uzbek Latin uses 'iy' suffix for relational adjectives where Persian uses ī (Haqiqiy for Persian Ḥaqīqī).
Uzbek may pluralize ethnonyms with -lar suffix (Qoraqoyunlilar = the Qaraqoyunlu people).

uzbek_cyrillic_soviet_legacy.description: Uzbek Cyrillic (Soviet-era 1940-1995, still in widespread use particularly in catalogues for pre-1995 records). Adds letters Ҳ (for /h/), Қ (for /q/), Ғ (for /ɣ/), Ў (for /o/), Ё (for /jo/).
indexing_notes: Uzbek Cyrillic Ҳ (for /h/) is distinct from Russian Cyrillic Х (for /x/); the Soviet-era Az Cyrillic Һ is a third variant.
Uzbek Cyrillic Қ corresponds to Persian ق; same as Az Cyrillic Г and Russian Cyrillic К-К compound.
Uzbek Cyrillic Ж corresponds to Persian ج /dʒ/; same as Russian Cyrillic Дж digraph, Az Cyrillic Ҹ.

tajik_cyrillic_modern.description: Tajik Cyrillic (since 1939, officially Tajikistan's script although Persian-Arabic script is also used informally). Adds letters Ҷ (for /dʒ/), Ҳ (for /h/), Ғ (for /ɣ/), Қ (for /q/), Ӣ (for long /iː/), Ӯ (for long /uː/). The long-vowel diacritics are the distinguishing feature.
applies_to: Rudaki Institute of Language and Literature Dushanbe
indexing_notes: Tajik Cyrillic adds the diacritic Ӣ for long /iː/ vowel, distinguishing Ҳақиқӣ from a hypothetical Ҳақиқи with short /i/. This is a feature of Tajik that Uzbek and Russian lack.
Tajik Cyrillic Ҷ corresponds to Persian ج, same as Uzbek Cyrillic Ж and Az Cyrillic Ҹ, distinct from Russian Дж.
Tajik's Persian-substrate makes its transliteration of Persian proper names more conservative (closer to original Persian orthography) than Uzbek's.

devanagari_south_asian.description: Devanagari script transliteration used in some South Asian (particularly Indian Hindu/Hindi-medium) catalogue entries for Persian manuscripts. Most South Asian Persian mss are catalogued in Persian/Urdu nastaʿliq (see urdu_nastaliq below); Devanagari versions appear primarily in Hindi-language scholarly literature.
indexing_notes: Devanagari with nukta (e.g., क़ for /q/, ज़ for /z/, फ़ for /f/) distinguishes Arabic-origin sounds from native Hindi phonology.
Hindi catalogues often drop the nukta (काराकोयुनलू vs. क़ाराक़ोयुनलू); both forms may co-exist in indexing.

The Devanagari forms are most likely to appear in Hindi-language descriptions of Salar Jang or other Indian institutional holdings, NOT in the catalogue records themselves which are typically in Urdu nastaʿliq.

urdu_nastaliq_south_asian.description: Urdu nastaʿliq script (essentially Persian-Arabic script with minor Indic adaptations). Used in most South Asian Persian/Urdu manuscript catalogues. The Arabic-script base is identical to Persian (covered by v1.0 search_keys.json arabic_script family), but Urdu uses ہ (do-chashmi he) where Persian uses ه, and may use slightly different diacritic conventions.
indexing_notes: Urdu nastaʿliq uses ہ (Unicode U+06C1, do-chashmi he) for the /h/ sound that Persian writes as ه (Unicode U+0647); this is a critical distinction for Indian institutional catalogue search, as the two characters do not always match in fulltext search.
Urdu may also use ی (Unicode U+06CC, Arabic farsi yeh) where Persian uses ي (Unicode U+064A, Arabic yeh) or ی; Persian-script search must accommodate Unicode variant matching.
South Asian catalogues often interleave Persian and Urdu forms in the same record; search batteries should test both ه/ہ and ی/ي variants.

yusuf_hakiki_homonym_treatment.purpose: Across all 9 script families in `script_families` above, the homonym question 'is this Cihanşah Hakîkî or Yusuf Hakîkî?' must be resolved per-hit using the standard v1.0 disambiguation criteria.
disambiguation_criteria: Date filter: Cihanşah Qaraqoyunlu d. 872 AH / 1467 CE. Yusuf Hakîkî Bursavi d. 893 AH / 1487 CE (Ottoman, Bursa-based, Hurufi-adjacent). A record dated post-872 AH that names 'Hakîkî' without further qualifier is more likely Yusuf Hakîkî unless the codex content positively confirms Cihanşah's Dīvān.

Context cluster: Cihanşah's Hakîkî pen-name appears with Qaraqoyunlu / Karakoyunlu titulature and Persian-Turkic bilingual content. Yusuf Hakîkî appears with Ottoman / Bursa context and Hurufi-symbolic content.

Hurufi sub-cluster: both poets are Hurufi-adjacent (per the v1.0 mecmûʿa-adjacency-Nesîmî diagnostic), but Yusuf Hakîkî's Hurufi affiliation is more direct (he is documented as a Bektashi-Hurufi initiate).
